worth_it_explanation: IAS brand safety and suitability settings are not a one-time configuration. They control which content categories and risk levels the IAS pre-bid segment will block before an impression is purchased. An account that has never revisited its suitability profile may be blocking more inventory than the brand actually needs to avoid, which limits reach and raises cost-per-impression, or it may be too permissive, placing ads next to content the brand has said it wants no part of. These checks require nothing beyond IAS Signal and a conversation with the brand about its documented risk tolerance. The fix when something is wrong is a settings change, not a campaign rebuild.
intro:
Integral Ad Science (IAS) brand safety and suitability controls determine which content environments the IAS pre-bid segment will block before an impression is purchased. IAS classifies inventory across a set of sensitive content categories, such as adult content, violence, hate speech, and illegal drugs, and assigns each piece of inventory a risk level within each category: low, moderate, high, or very high. An advertiser's suitability profile specifies which categories to monitor and where to draw the risk threshold for each.

IAS also offers Context Control, a contextual avoidance and targeting product that classifies pages by topic, sentiment, and emotional tone using natural language processing. Context Control operates alongside the category-and-risk-level controls: the category settings block content based on sensitive subject matter, while Context Control can further refine which pages are suitable based on the surrounding editorial context.

These checks confirm that the suitability profile reflects a deliberate decision by the brand, that the enabled categories and risk thresholds match the brand's documented risk tolerance, and that a single suitability profile has not been applied across campaigns with meaningfully different audience or brand-risk requirements.

audit_checks.title: IAS suitability profile has not been tailored to the brand and may be too permissive or too restrictive
audit_checks.impact_rationale: A suitability profile left at default settings reflects IAS's initial configuration, not a deliberate choice by the brand or its agency. Default settings may block more inventory than the brand needs to avoid, reducing reach and raising costs, or they may leave content categories active that the brand has said are off-limits. Either way, the account's suitability posture does not reflect the brand's actual requirements.

educate.base:
IAS classifies inventory across a set of sensitive content categories and assigns each a risk level: low, moderate, high, or very high. An advertiser's suitability profile specifies which categories to activate and where to draw the risk threshold for each one. The risk levels work as thresholds: selecting 'high risk' for violence means the pre-bid segment will block inventory IAS has scored as high or very high on violence, while allowing inventory scored as low or moderate. Selecting 'low risk' blocks far more inventory across that same category.

IAS provides default suitability settings when an account is first configured. Those defaults represent a starting point, not a recommendation calibrated to the brand. A profile that has never been reviewed since initial setup may reflect whatever the account team entered during onboarding, which may not match the brand's current risk tolerance or campaign goals.

A few signs suggest the suitability profile has not been tailored:
  • The active suitability settings in IAS Signal match the out-of-the-box defaults with no record of a review or adjustment
  • The brand has a documented safety policy or brand brief, but the IAS settings do not map to any of its stated categories or thresholds
  • The IAS account team cannot identify when the suitability profile was last reviewed or by whom
You may want to request the brand's documented risk tolerance or safety brief before comparing it against the current IAS suitability configuration. IAS suitability categories align with the framework of sensitive content topics maintained by the IAB Tech Lab Content Taxonomy, which provides a shared vocabulary for discussing brand safety and suitability categories across the industry.

site_type_overlays.ecommerce: For a retail advertiser, the categories that warrant specific attention are typically violence, hate speech, and illegal drugs. You may want to check whether the risk thresholds for those categories reflect the brand's audience. A brand that sells products to families may need lower thresholds than a brand that sells to adults, even within the same category. Adult content and explicit material categories are worth confirming as active regardless of the brand's audience.
site_type_overlays.lead_gen: For a lead-generation advertiser, brand proximity to content in the crime, misinformation, and sensitive social issues categories can affect form completion rates beyond the direct reputational question. You may want to confirm that the suitability profile activates those categories at a threshold the brand is comfortable with, rather than relying on the default threshold, which may not reflect how risk-sensitive the brand actually is.

learn_more.source_title: About Integral Ad Science's media quality verification

educate.learn_more_note: Detailed documentation on IAS suitability profile configuration, the full list of content categories, and default versus custom threshold settings is available in the IAS advertiser help center. Access requires an active IAS account login at helpcenter.integralplatform.com. Your IAS account team can walk through the current profile settings and compare them against the brand's risk tolerance as part of a standard account review.

investigate.intro: Review the active suitability profile in IAS Signal and compare it against any documented brand safety policy or risk brief.

ias_signal.label: IAS Signal
steps: Log in to IAS Signal and navigate to the brand safety and suitability settings for the advertiser account.
Review the list of active content categories. Note which categories are enabled, which are disabled, and what risk threshold is set for each active category.
Ask the brand or account team whether a brand safety brief, risk tolerance document, or suitability policy exists. If one does, compare the active IAS categories and thresholds against the document.

Confirm with the IAS account team or in the account history whether the suitability profile has been reviewed since the account was first configured. Note the date of the last documented review if available.

Record any category that is active in IAS but not mentioned in the brand's policy, and any category called out in the policy but not active or set to a more permissive threshold than the policy requires.

evaluate.logic: The practitioner records the active categories and their risk thresholds from IAS Signal and compares them against the brand's documented risk tolerance or safety policy. An account where the active categories and thresholds match a current, reviewed brand policy passes. An account where the settings have never been reviewed since initial configuration, or where the active settings cannot be mapped to any documented brand decision, is a fail recorded with the specific gaps between the settings and the policy.
evaluate.fallback_message: Record the active IAS suitability categories and thresholds above to compare against the brand's policy.

generate.narrative_template: {{voice_found}} that {{site_name}}'s IAS suitability profile has not been reviewed against the brand's documented risk tolerance since the account was initially configured. The active category settings and risk thresholds reflect the initial setup rather than a deliberate decision calibrated to the brand's requirements, which means the account may be blocking inventory the brand would accept or accepting content categories the brand has said it wants to avoid. {{voice_recommend}} comparing the current IAS category settings and risk thresholds against the brand's safety policy, adjusting the thresholds to match the documented tolerance, and scheduling a review of the suitability profile at least once per quarter as campaign goals and brand context evolve.

audit_checks.title: Enabled IAS suitability categories and risk levels do not reflect the brand's documented risk tolerance
audit_checks.impact_rationale: When the active suitability categories or risk thresholds in IAS Signal contradict the brand's documented policy, ads run next to content the brand has explicitly said is off-limits, or the account blocks content the brand is comfortable with, reducing reach without a corresponding safety benefit. The gap between stated policy and active settings is the most direct form of suitability failure in an IAS deployment.

educate.base:
IAS brand suitability categories map to the sensitive content topics maintained by the IAB Tech Lab Content Taxonomy, which provides a shared vocabulary for brand safety and suitability classification across the advertising industry. Categories include adult content, violence, hate speech, illegal drugs, alcohol and tobacco, gambling, profanity, misinformation, and sensitive social issues, among others. Within each category, IAS assigns a risk level to each piece of inventory: low, moderate, high, or very high. An advertiser's suitability profile sets the threshold for each category, blocking inventory at or above that threshold.

The relationship between the threshold setting and what actually gets blocked matters. Setting a category to 'high risk' means the pre-bid segment blocks inventory IAS has scored as high or very high within that category, but passes inventory scored as low or moderate. Setting the same category to 'low risk' blocks a much larger portion of available inventory. A brand that sets all categories to their most permissive threshold is not protected against moderate-risk content, even though moderate-risk content may include material the brand's own policy says it wants to avoid.

A few signs point to a mismatch between the active settings and the brand's documented policy:
  • A category the brand has explicitly flagged as off-limits is disabled in IAS Signal or set to a threshold that allows moderate-risk inventory through
  • A category is set to its most restrictive threshold with no documented reason, blocking content the brand would accept and reducing available inventory
  • The suitability configuration was built by an agency or previous account team and has not been reviewed against the current brand brief
You may want to review the brand's most recent safety brief or annual brand policy document before evaluating the IAS settings, as policies can change across campaign cycles.

site_type_overlays.ecommerce: For a retail advertiser, mismatches are most consequential in categories directly tied to product reputation, such as fake news, crime, and hate speech. A brand that sells consumer goods may face different stakeholder expectations than one that sells professional services, so you may want to confirm whether the active thresholds reflect the brand's actual audience context and not just an industry-standard default.
site_type_overlays.lead_gen: For a lead-generation advertiser, a threshold mismatch in the misinformation or sensitive social issues categories can place the brand's form or landing-page ad next to content that reduces credibility with the exact audience the campaign is trying to convert. You may want to cross-reference the suitability settings against recent brand feedback or any client-provided list of content adjacencies to avoid.

learn_more.source_title: About Integral Ad Science's media quality verification

educate.learn_more_note: The full list of IAS suitability categories, the definition of each risk threshold, and guidance on selecting thresholds appropriate to a brand's policy are documented in the IAS advertiser help center at helpcenter.integralplatform.com. Access requires an active IAS account login. Your IAS account team can pull the current settings from Signal and walk through the threshold definitions category by category.

impactful_updates.update_title: GARM brand safety framework discontinued in 2024
impactful_updates.update_description: The Global Alliance for Responsible Media (GARM) and its Brand Safety Floor and Suitability Framework were discontinued in August 2024 when the World Federation of Advertisers disbanded GARM. IAS suitability categories remain grounded in the underlying sensitive content taxonomy maintained by the IAB Tech Lab, which continues to publish and update the Content Taxonomy as an active standard. Advertisers reviewing their IAS settings should reference the IAB Tech Lab Content Taxonomy rather than the GARM framework documentation.

investigate.intro: Map the brand's documented risk tolerance or safety policy to the active IAS category settings in Signal, noting where any category is set more permissively or more restrictively than the policy requires.

ias_signal.label: IAS Signal
steps: Log in to IAS Signal and open the suitability settings for the advertiser account. Export or photograph the full list of active categories and their risk thresholds.
Obtain the brand's current safety brief, risk tolerance document, or any written guidance from the client about content adjacencies they want to avoid.

For each category listed in the brand's policy, confirm whether that category is active in IAS Signal and whether the risk threshold set in IAS matches the tolerance stated in the policy. Record any category where the IAS threshold is more permissive than the policy requires.

For each category active in IAS Signal but not addressed in the brand's policy, note it for discussion with the client. The category may represent an additional protection the brand did not think to specify, or it may be blocking inventory without a documented rationale.
Note whether the suitability settings were last modified by the current account team or by a previous team, and whether a review occurred after the most recent brand brief was issued.

evaluate.logic: The practitioner records each IAS category and threshold alongside the corresponding guidance in the brand's documented policy. Categories where the IAS threshold matches or is more restrictive than the policy pass. Categories where the IAS threshold is more permissive than the policy requires are a fail, recorded with the category name, the current threshold, and what the policy calls for. Categories active in IAS with no corresponding policy guidance are noted for client discussion but are not automatically a fail.
evaluate.fallback_message: Record the IAS suitability categories and thresholds alongside the brand's policy guidance above to see the evaluation.

generate.narrative_template: {{voice_found}} a mismatch between {{site_name}}'s IAS suitability configuration in Signal and the brand's documented risk tolerance. Specific content categories are set to thresholds that allow inventory through that the brand's policy identifies as off-limits, meaning the IAS pre-bid segment does not block the content the brand has said it wants to avoid. {{voice_recommend}} mapping each active IAS category setting to the corresponding guidance in the brand's safety brief, tightening thresholds where the current setting is more permissive than the policy requires, and documenting any intentional deviations so the rationale is preserved for future reviews.

audit_checks.title: One suitability profile is applied across campaigns with different risk requirements
audit_checks.impact_rationale: A single suitability profile applied to campaigns with meaningfully different audiences or risk contexts leaves at least one of those campaigns either over-restricted or under-protected. A brand-awareness campaign running on premium video and a prospecting campaign running across the open web typically require different suitability thresholds; treating them as identical assumes a uniformity of risk that rarely holds.

educate.base:
IAS suitability settings can be configured at different levels within an account, allowing the account to maintain distinct suitability profiles for campaigns that serve different audiences, run in different environments, or carry different risk tolerances on behalf of the same brand.

A high-reach open-web display campaign and a connected television (CTV) brand-awareness buy do not face the same content adjacency risks. Display on the open web runs across a wide range of publishers and user-generated content platforms where suitability categories like profanity, misinformation, and sensitive social issues are more likely to surface. CTV inventory is typically more curated, but the categories and thresholds that matter may differ, particularly for violence or mature content on streaming platforms that carry both family-friendly and adult programming.

When a single suitability profile governs all campaigns, one of two problems can arise. If the shared profile is calibrated for the most sensitive campaign, it may block more inventory than the less sensitive campaigns need to avoid, reducing reach and raising costs without a safety benefit. If the shared profile is calibrated for the least sensitive campaign, the more sensitive campaigns run under settings that would not pass the brand's own review if applied deliberately.

A few signs point to a single profile governing dissimilar campaigns:
  • All active campaigns share the same IAS segment ID or suitability configuration with no documented reason for the uniformity
  • A campaign targeting families or children runs under the same suitability threshold as a campaign targeting adults with no adjustment for the audience difference
  • Campaigns running in CTV, video, and open-web display all use the same thresholds, despite the different content environments those formats represent
You may want to map the active campaigns by audience and environment before evaluating whether a shared suitability profile is appropriate or whether separate configurations are warranted.

site_type_overlays.ecommerce: A retailer running a children's toy campaign alongside an adult apparel or beverage campaign on the same IAS account setup may find that one profile fits neither campaign well. You may want to confirm whether distinct suitability configurations exist for campaigns with different age-audience requirements, or whether both are governed by a single shared profile that may be mis-calibrated for at least one of them.
site_type_overlays.lead_gen: For a lead-generation advertiser running acquisition campaigns across multiple verticals, such as financial services and consumer software, the content adjacencies that carry risk for one product line may be less concerning for another. You may want to confirm whether the IAS suitability configuration for each campaign reflects the vertical's specific risk context rather than a single shared profile applied across all lead-gen activity.

learn_more.source_title: About Integral Ad Science's media quality verification

educate.learn_more_note: Guidance on creating and managing distinct suitability profiles for different campaigns, and on how IAS Signal organizes suitability settings by advertiser and campaign, is available in the IAS advertiser help center at helpcenter.integralplatform.com. Access requires an active IAS account login. Your IAS account team can review the current configuration and advise on whether separate profiles are appropriate for the active campaign mix.

investigate.intro: Map the active suitability configuration for each campaign and confirm whether campaigns with different audience or risk contexts carry appropriately distinct settings.

ias_signal.label: IAS Signal
steps: Log in to IAS Signal and review the active campaigns or ad sets. Note which suitability profile or segment is assigned to each campaign.

Identify whether any two campaigns with different audience targets, environments, or risk requirements share the same suitability configuration. Record any cases where a family or children's audience campaign shares settings with an adult audience campaign.

For campaigns running in different environments, such as connected television (CTV), video, and open-web display, note whether the suitability thresholds differ or whether a single shared setting governs all environments.
Ask the account team or client whether the shared suitability configuration was a deliberate decision documented anywhere, or whether it reflects the original account setup that was never revisited.

For any campaign where the shared profile appears mis-calibrated, note the campaign name, the audience it serves, and the specific category or threshold that appears inconsistent with the campaign's risk context.

evaluate.logic: The practitioner maps the active suitability configuration to each campaign and notes where campaigns with different audience or environment contexts share a single setting. Campaigns that share a suitability profile and have documented justification for that uniformity pass. Campaigns that share a profile because the account was never configured to distinguish between them, particularly where one campaign serves a more restricted audience than another, are a fail recorded with the campaign names and the specific risk context difference that the shared profile fails to account for.
evaluate.fallback_message: List the active campaigns and their suitability configurations above to see the evaluation.

generate.narrative_template: {{voice_found}} that {{site_name}}'s IAS suitability configuration applies the same settings across campaigns with meaningfully different audience and content-environment contexts. Campaigns that serve different audiences, or that run in connected television (CTV) versus open-web display environments, are governed by a single suitability profile that is not calibrated to the requirements of each. {{voice_recommend}} reviewing the active campaign mix and creating distinct IAS suitability configurations where campaigns differ in audience, environment, or documented risk tolerance, so each campaign operates under settings appropriate to its specific context rather than the shared average.
